solution: 
```java
class SORTracker {
    private PriorityQueue<Location> maxHeap;
    private PriorityQueue<Location> minHeap;
    private int getCallCount;

    public SORTracker() {
        maxHeap = new PriorityQueue<>((a, b) -> a.compareTo(b));
        minHeap = new PriorityQueue<>((a, b) -> b.compareTo(a));
        getCallCount = 0;
    }

    public void add(String name, int score) {
        maxHeap.offer(new Location(name, score));
        minHeap.offer(maxHeap.poll());
    }

    public String get() {
        getCallCount++;
        Location result = minHeap.poll();
        maxHeap.offer(result);
        return result.name;
    }

    private class Location implements Comparable<Location> {
        String name;
        int score;

        Location(String name, int score) {
            this.name = name;
            this.score = score;
        }

        public int compareTo(Location other) {
            if (this.score != other.score) {
                return other.score - this.score;
            }
            return this.name.compareTo(other.name);
        }
    }
}
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(log n) for add and get
space complexity: O(n)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution uses two priority queues (heaps) to maintain the locations efficiently. The maxHeap stores the best locations, and the minHeap stores the rest. When adding a location, it is placed in the maxHeap and then moved to the minHeap. When retrieving the i-th best location, the top of the minHeap is returned and moved to the maxHeap. This ensures efficient insertion and retrieval with logarithmic time complexity.
